Tafseer:
The divine law governing the success of the call to truth:
We explained in detail in the previous Surah the mental anxiety and distress that overtook the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) before his mission—while searching for the truth—and during the early phase of his mission, due to the intense opposition and scarcity of helpers. At the same time, he was given the glad tiding that the difficulties he faced were temporary, Allah Almighty would soon remove them, and the future of his call would be far brighter than its past. Later, when the light of divine revelation cleared his anxiety and the truth became manifest, Allah Almighty—for his instruction and further encouragement—explained His established law governing the success of the call to truth, which every caller to truth must face, and which serves as the core theme of this Surah:
(Indeed, with hardship, there is ease) (verse 5).
Expanding the breast (sharh-i-sadr) means blessing with the insight and gnosis that are the fruits of true faith. It is this insight that produces the trust and reliance on Allah Almighty that serve as the source of all strength and resolve. If this faith is present, no opposition can shake a person's resolve; and if it is absent, a person can suffer defeat even without any opposition.

'And We removed from you your burden' (wa wada'na 'anka wizrak) - Grammatically, this is connected to the preceding sentence, and therefore we translated it under that. This style was explained under Surah An-Naba.

Grief upon grief! This describes the 'burden' (wizr)—showing that it weighed heavily on your back. This is the same burden of grief that he experienced before his mission because he was parched and searching for the truth without finding any clue. When Allah opened the path of guidance, this grief was doubled because his entire nation rose as his enemy.
Describing this grief as 'weighed heavily on your back' (anqada dhahrak) is not an exaggeration, but the absolute truth. Since the guidance with which Allah expanded your breast had entered your heart, it was natural for you to expect that it should enter every heart. But contrary to this expectation, when you saw that the more your efforts increased, the more people ran away from the call, you naturally assumed that there was some shortcoming in your efforts because of which the call was not making an impact. This assumption made you increase your efforts even more, but when even that did not change the situation, your anxiety doubled. Furthermore, during such circumstances, if the revelation was delayed, this delay would increase your grief, fearing it might be due to Allah's displeasure. To remove his anxiety, he was comforted here as well as in Surah Ta-Ha:
(Ta-Ha. We have not revealed to you the Qur'an to cause you distress, but only as a reminder for those who fear [Allah]) (Ta-Ha, 20:1-3).

That is: is it not a fact that We raised high your reputation! 'Laka' (for you) is used here—just as in the first verse—to express specification, support, and help. That is: to strengthen and encourage you, Allah Almighty spread your reputation far and wide.
The call spreading far and wide: This verse helps determine the period of the Surah's revelation—showing that it was revealed when your call had begun to spread across Arabia. It should be noted that while the leaders of Mecca, whom the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) addressed first, persisted in opposition, the pilgrims who arrived during the Hajj season carried this call to the outskirts of Mecca, specifically to the Helpers (Ansar) of Medina. Gradually, it reached not only the distant Arab tribes but also neighboring countries, making it clear that this voice would not be silenced; rather, a time was coming when it would be on everyone's tongue, and every corner would echo with 'Allahu Akbar'.

The primary lesson: This is the primary lesson intended to be delivered in the light of the aforementioned proofs, serving as the core theme of the Surah. It is stated: since you are witnessing this conduct of Allah with you, remain assured of His law—namely, He has bound 'ease' (yusr) with 'difficulty' ('usr'). That is: ease will surely manifest, but after passing through the period of trial. In the previous Surah, this reality was proved from nature and the Prophet's life experiences. In this Surah, his own life experiences are presented as proofs to make it more effective.
A specific point: Note that the same point is stated twice. This repetition is not merely for emphasis, as commonly assumed, but to show that 'difficulty' and 'ease' are interconnected in this world. When a person breaks through one difficult pass, he should not assume that he will never face another; rather, a second and third pass can also appear, and he must maintain the courage to break through them. Life is a continuous struggle. In this world, every traveler must face high and low paths, and only after passing through them does he reach his destination. This is Allah's way with those who choose the path of truth. He does not remove all obstacles on their own, but they themselves must struggle to remove them. However, His guarantee is that if they do not lose hope and use whatever strength He has blessed them with, He will create ease after every difficulty, refreshing their strength and resolve for the subsequent journey, until they reach their destination by overcoming one difficulty after another.
The wisdom behind this trial is explained in the Qur'an: in this manner, Allah distinguishes between the hypocrite and the sincere, the truthful and the show-off, so that everyone receives reward or punishment in accordance with their deeds, leaving no room for anyone to complain of injustice. Without this trial, the distinction between the true and the false could not have been made.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 5 and 6 was combined in the original text.]

Striving for the final destination: This is the instruction to strive for the final destination. 'Nasaba yansabu' means to strive, exert effort, and work hard. It is stated: having overcome the obstacles on the path, when the stage arrives that Allah's help becomes visible, Mecca is conquered, the enemies surrender, and people enter Islam in multitudes, then gird up your loins and work hard to prepare for the final destination—namely, meeting your Lord. Thus, two things are combined in these verses:
- First, the glad tiding that you will succeed in overcoming all obstacles and reaching the final destination, completing your great responsibility with success.
- Second, having reached the final destination, the time has not come to relax; rather, you must work hard to prepare for the journey of meeting your Lord.
In executing this final instruction, the Prophet's dedication to worship increased greatly during the final phase of his life. Seeing him exert such effort, some asked: 'O Messenger of Allah, your past and future sins have been forgiven, so why do you exert such effort?' He responded:
(Should I not then be a grateful servant?)
This is stated briefly here, and its complete details will appear in Surah An-Nasr. We reproduce the Surah here:
(When the victory of Allah has come and the conquest, and you see the people entering into the religion of Allah in multitudes, then exalt [Him] with praise of your Lord and ask forgiveness of Him. Indeed, He is ever Accepting of repentance.) (An-Nasr, 110:1-3).
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 7 and 8 was combined in the original text.]
